body.title: [triage] The inference corpus RECORDS SUCCESSES AND FORGETS FAILURES — all 70 of today's errors are byte-identical (`{'raw': None}`, elapsed_ms=0). The system of record has the same non-injective defect as everything it records.
body.intention: I intend that the inference corpus preserves the CAUSE and the COST of a failure, because a failure is the datum worth keeping — a record that says only 'something went wrong' has recorded the shape of the truth and thrown away the truth. [intention_date: 2026-07-14]
body.description:
**AKIEN, 2026-07-14:** *"every inference call is supposed to be tracked, logged both the up
and downstream, so you should be able to go and inspect those... that was the intention."*

**HE IS RIGHT, AND I SHOULD HAVE LOOKED.** `~/.unseen_university/inference_corpus/YYYYMMDD.io.jsonl` exists,
carries real model names, and had the answer to yesterday's disaster sitting in it the entire time:

    20260714 io corpus, rich-schema calls:
      ok     n=62   p50=6.8s   p95=237.4s   max=284.8s      <-- THE SATURATION. Written down.
      error  n=70   p50=0.0s   p95=0.0s     max=0.0s

A p95 of **237 seconds** on SUCCESSFUL calls is the queue, recorded, in a file. One read of `elapsed_ms` and
the 2026-07-13 saturation would have been obvious in seconds. Instead I reasoned from sockets and produced
two confidently-wrong diagnoses. **I went hunting for evidence I already had.** That is a lesson about me
([[feedback_account_for_your_own_load]]).

**BUT THE CORPUS HAS THE SAME DISEASE AS EVERYTHING IT RECORDS, AND THAT IS THIS TICKET.**

  * **`elapsed_ms == 0` for EVERY error.** A call that burned 60 seconds and timed out is recorded as having
    taken **zero milliseconds**. The corpus therefore *systematically erases the cost of failure* — and every
    latency or budget analysis built on it is wrong, in a known direction, by the exact amount that matters.
  * **`response == "{'raw': None}"` for ALL 70 errors — BYTE-IDENTICAL.** A parse failure, a timeout, a
    refused connection, and a reasoning model truncated at `finish_reason='length'` all collapse into one
    string with the cause erased.

**THIS IS THE SAME NON-INJECTIVE MAP, IN THE SYSTEM OF RECORD.** It is the crash wearing CP1's clothes
(T-intent-extractor-crash-masquerades-as-refusal), the saturated ollama reporting healthy
(T-healthmonitor-probes-reachability-not-capability), and INDETERMINATE collapsing into GREEN — except here
it is in the artifact whose ONE JOB is to preserve causes. **The corpus keeps its successes and forgets why
anything failed. That is exactly backwards: the failures are the data (CP2).**

**AND A SECOND, DISJOINT GAP — the run-record path is dark for real traffic.**
`devices/inference/run_record.py` writes rich per-run "inference tickets"
(`~/.unseen_university/inference_corpus/runs/`). **804 of them exist. NOT ONE NAMES A REAL MODEL.** Every
recorded hop is `stub-model`, `stub`, or `''` — the entire run-record corpus is TEST OUTPUT. Cause:
`RunRecord` is written only by `BaseDomain.run`; **`InferenceDevice.dispatch()` has ZERO references to it**,
and `dispatch()` is the path the intent extractor (`self._get_inference().dispatch(req)`), aider, and DS all
take. So the run-record corpus *looks* alive — 804 records, growing daily, resolutions spread plausibly
across done/harvest_wall/capability_ceiling — and is **entirely fictional**. A corpus indistinguishable from
a working one is worse than an empty one: it does not merely fail to inform, it actively misleads.

**Third, and it is Akien's contamination argument exactly:** the TEST SUITE writes into the PRODUCTION
corpus (326,694 fabricated tokens). `--unshare-net` does not stop this, because it is a FILESYSTEM write, not
a network call. The tester needs corpus/FS isolation too, not only network isolation
(T-tester-owns-the-network).

**Scope boundary:** IN — (a) an error row records its CAUSE (named class: parse / timeout / refused /
truncated / no-capacity) and its real `elapsed_ms`; (b) `dispatch()` writes a run record, so the real traffic
is recorded and not just the domain path; (c) the test suite must not write to the production corpus. OUT —
the distributional monitor over the corpus (worth its own ticket: a 48% error rate went unnoticed today, and
only the DISTRIBUTION screams when every record is well-formed).

**Test plan / discriminating fixture:** four failures — a parse error, a 60s timeout, a refused connection,
and a `finish_reason='length'` truncation — must land as FOUR DISTINCT records, each carrying its cause and
its true elapsed time. **A build that writes `{'raw': None}` with `elapsed_ms=0` for all four fails, which is
the entire point.** The tester's new network fixtures (`saturated`, `truncated_reasoner`, `array_answer`)
make all four producible on demand — this ticket is buildable *because* of T-tester-owns-the-network.

**Completion criteria:**
  1. Four different failure causes -> four different records. Asserted, not assumed.
  2. A failed call's `elapsed_ms` reflects the time it actually burned. A 60s timeout is not 0ms.
  3. `dispatch()` writes a run record; a real model name appears in the run corpus for the first time.
  4. A test run cannot write to the production corpus.
